howItWorks.intro: This calculator generates a composite wildfire risk score (0-100) by weighting six key factors that determine a property's vulnerability to wildfire damage.
rules: Vegetation type contributes 30% of the score — dense dry brush and conifers score highest risk
Terrain slope contributes 20% — fire travels faster uphill, doubling speed for every 20% increase in slope
Climate zone contributes 15% — arid and drought-prone regions have higher ignition and spread risk
Defensible space contributes 15% — CAL FIRE recommends 100 feet of defensible space in high-risk zones
Roof material contributes 10% — ember exposure is the leading cause of structure ignition during wildfires
Road access contributes 10% — affects both evacuation safety and firefighter response time
howItWorks.outro: This is an educational risk estimate. For official risk assessments, consult your local fire department, CAL FIRE hazard maps, or a certified wildfire risk assessor. Insurance companies use proprietary models that may weight factors differently.

faq.question: How does South Dakota handle this differently?
faq.answer: South Dakota has its own specific rules, rates, and limits that may differ significantly from federal guidelines or other states. This calculator uses South Dakota-specific data where available. Always verify with a local professional for important decisions.

faq.question: What is a good wildfire risk score?
faq.answer: Scores under 30 indicate low risk, 30-50 moderate risk, 50-70 high risk, and above 70 extreme risk. Most properties in wildland-urban interface areas score 40-70. The goal is to reduce controllable factors like defensible space and building materials.

faq.question: How much defensible space do I need?
faq.answer: CAL FIRE requires 100 feet of defensible space around structures in State Responsibility Areas. Zone 1 (0-30 feet) should be lean, clean, and green with no combustible material. Zone 2 (30-100 feet) should have reduced and spaced vegetation. Some communities require up to 200 feet.

faq.question: Does wildfire risk affect my insurance?
faq.answer: Yes, significantly. Homes in high-risk areas may face premiums 2-5x higher than low-risk homes, or may be dropped by insurers entirely. California's FAIR Plan provides last-resort coverage. Demonstrating defensible space and fire-resistant materials can help reduce premiums.

faq.question: What roof material is best for wildfire?
faq.answer: Class A rated roofing (metal, concrete tile, clay tile, slate) offers the best fire protection. Asphalt composition shingles are Class A or B. Wood shake and shingle roofs are the most vulnerable. Replacing a wood shake roof is one of the most impactful mitigation steps.

faq.question: How does slope affect wildfire spread?
faq.answer: Fire moves faster uphill because flames preheat upslope fuels. For every 10-degree increase in slope, fire spread rate roughly doubles. A fire on a 30% slope can move 2-4 times faster than on flat ground. Homes on ridgetops and steep hillsides face elevated risk from below.
